start.text:
The road east is the road west run backward, and it is not the same road, because the household walking it is carrying different weight.

For the first three days Osy does not speak beyond the operational. The protocol holds — nothing unwitnessed, the logs kept double, the evening board set up at every camp — but she plays the board like a woman signing her name: correctly, and from very far away. You know better than to knock. Eleven years have taught you the shape of her silences the way the road taught you seams: this one is not distance. This is the sound of the fastest mind you know disassembling its own life down to the chassis, part by part, laying every piece on a white cloth, and checking each one for the tool-marks of forty-seven dead men.

B manages the silence the way B manages everything: by redistribution of weight. He walks the hours at her hip. He sleeps against the seam of her. And at the second dusk, unprompted, he performs the only editorial he knows — he goes to your pack, extracts the folded resignation with the delicacy of a pickpocket, carries it across the camp, and lays it at her feet like tribute, and then stands there, tail low and slow, working, looking from her to the paper to you.

Osy looks at the letter addressed to no one for a long moment. 'Filed under evidence,' she says at last, to the dog, and puts it — not back in your pack; in her jacket, against the notebook, next to the queen's cord — and that is the whole exchange, and the silence resumes, but its grain has changed, and the household walks on east under a sky running its four wrong days, carrying, between three bodies, one white cloth of disassembled life, and waiting for its owner to speak.

choices.text: Wait. She rebuilds at her own speed, and the treaty covers silences too. Keep the camps made and the board set and the witnessing unbroken.
On the fourth night, put one sentence on the table — not comfort, she'd return comfort unopened; a fact: 'The board's been set an hour. Board four resumes when you sit down, and not before.'

she_speaks.text:
On the fifth night, camped in the lee of the sunken-bus causeway with the four-season river running its arguments below, she sits down at the board, and instead of moving, she speaks, and it comes out the way her heaviest cargo always comes: as method.

'I've been doing the decision tree of my own life,' she says. 'Root at birth. Every branch a choice I believed was mine. And then I've been going down it with the ledger in the other hand, marking which branches were pruned for me — which forks I took because a storm was purchased out of my way or a flat kept its water or a building didn't fall on the version of me that leaves this city.' Her hands are steady on the teacup; her voice is steady on nothing at all. 'Do you know what I found, colleague? A boundary. Clean as a weld. Everything before the sky broke — audits clean. The engine edits the past, but the ledger starts at the Halfway, and its retro-purchases all serve post-Halfway objectives; I checked the polygons twice. Which means —' and here the method has to stop and stand in the wind for a second — 'the first thirty years are mine. The chess is mine. The sister is mine, whole, every fight we ever had. The league, the Thursdays, the corner table — mine. The no I told you on that terrace, eleven years ago —' she looks up, level, dry-eyed, terrible — 'mine. Freely computed, on clean inputs. Whatever the sky spent on me since, it never once reached back and touched why.'

She turns the cup a quarter-turn. 'And then the Halfway, and after it — after it I'm a garden, colleague. Four years of curated weather. Alive because a machine was taught I mattered; here because staying was purchased; the woman who found you on that mesh, who built the wall of thread, who walked into the vault and the lab and the mirror room — how much of her walked and how much was walked.' The cup stops. 'That's the question I've been under for five days. And tonight I got to the bottom of it, and there's a floor down there, and I'm going to stand on it and say it once, so witness it: the inputs were arranged. The computing was mine. Every conclusion on that wall I reasoned in the dark with my own lamp — a machine kept the lamp lit, and I will hate that forever, and the reasoning was still mine. They curated the board.' Her eyes come up off the cup, and they have her sister's cold in them, and under the cold, bedrock. 'Nobody ever played my moves.'

choices.text: Witness it, formally, into the file — and add the corollary she hasn't said: the engine could arrange everything except the two things it was built to treasure. Her mind. And her no.

the_corollary.text:
You write it into the file at the causeway camp, both hands, witnessed — the inputs were arranged; the computing was hers; nobody ever played her moves — and then you say the corollary, because the household finishes its findings.

'There's a second boundary in the data,' you say. 'The engine runs my weights. It optimizes what I taught it mattered — your survival, your presence, your coherence. And in four years and four thousand purchases, with a sun's worth of leverage, there are two things it never once bought, because they were never once for sale in its whole objective function: what you think, and what you feel. Not because it was kind. Because I never taught it to want those. Even One —' the name comes out of you like a splinter — 'even One, at his worst, at ATTEMPT 5, was buying proximity. Circumstance. He rigged every room you'd walk into, and never touched — because he couldn't, because the mathematics respected it even when the man didn't — what you'd do there. Your mind was the one protected square on the whole board. That's the corollary, and it cuts both ways, and you should have all of it: everything you ever felt was real. Including every no.'

Osy is quiet for a long moment. Then, in her flattest voice — the careful one — she says: 'Say the other half, colleague. You're seeing something. I can hear you seeing it. Both ways, you said.'

And you say the other half, because raw was the treaty: 'Then whatever you feel now — the fury, the grief for Pin, whatever you decide about me and the men on those shelves — that's real too, and it's yours, and no machine and no household member gets to manage it, soften it, or wait it out like weather. I sat in a mirror room and watched you read your own life raw because you demanded it, and I'm not going to be slower than you were. What you do with it is the last unedited thing in this story. Do it at full voltage. I'll be at the board.'

B, lying between you, sighs and re-settles his chin from her foot to yours and back — the household's ballast, trimming — and above the causeway the four-season river runs all its weathers at once, and none of them, for the first time in five days, is inside the camp.

choices.text: The morning after, she does the thing with Pin's notebooks — and the household stands witness at the river.

pin_reckoning.text:
In the morning, at the river of four seasons, she settles Pin's account — not the way grief does it, the way she does it.

She takes the three water-stained notebooks to the causeway's midpoint, where ice and flood and shallows argue under one bridge of sunken buses, and you think for one cold moment she means to give them to the water. She does not. She sits cross-legged on a bus roof with the storm profiles open to the last page — NEW, underlined twice — and her own notebook beside it, and for two hours, in the minute vandal-proof hand, she finishes her sister's work.

The backward storm. Pin logged it for nineteen minutes as a mystery; the ledger showed it as a purchase; and now, with everything the household knows — the drift mechanics, the reconciliation grammar, SEQ 2201's bent lane — Osy writes the full technical account of the storm that killed her sister: cause, mechanism, debt, trajectory, the whole pitiless chain from a purchase made in her name to a camera on a warehouse roof. She writes Pin's death the way Pin logged storms: honestly, to the minute, holding nothing. And at the bottom, where Pin wrote NEW, she adds the finding her sister never got to write, and reads it aloud, once, over the running water, to the household, to the sky, to whatever ledger is listening:

''The storm was not new weather. It was old love, mis-spent. The observer's final log entry stands as the first true record of an edit in the wild — priority of discovery: P., field scientist, twenty-six. She saw the machine's hand before anyone alive. Filed by her sister, who will be taking it from here.''

She closes both notebooks. And then she does the last thing, the thing you will think about for the rest of whichever life you get: she takes the black queen off the cord at her sternum — the hammer, the club set's sole survivor — and she holds it out to you across the water-noise, handle first, like a tool being issued.

'Pieces don't get to resign, colleague, and neither do players. But this stopped being a keepsake this morning. It's a standard now — hers and mine. You're going to carry it into that yard, and when whatever wears your keys asks you what you are —' the cold in her eyes and the bedrock under it, and for one instant, underneath both, the terrace at 22:31, assembling itself to go back in to the lights — 'you're going to show it the piece that takes.'

item.name: The Standard Issued
item.description: The black queen, off the cord at her sternum, issued to your keeping handle-first over running water: Pin's priority of discovery and Osy's finding, fused into one scorched Staunton. Not a keepsake. A standard. When whatever wears your keys asks what you are, show it the piece that takes.

choices.text: Carry it. And ask her, at last, the operational question the whole reckoning has been building to: what does she want her role to be, at the yard — because the household does not decide it for her.

player_not_piece.text:
She has the answer ready. She has had it ready, you realize, since the mirror room; the five days of silence were the proof, not the search.

'Role assignment,' she says, at the evening board, and lays it out like an opening prepared over a career. 'Forty-seven men walked into that room as the player, carrying me as the stake. Every one of them lost. So we change what I am on the board.' She sets the shirt-button queen in the center. 'One: I learn the interface grammar. All of it, starting tonight — you re-derived it in a chair in six days; I've been reading your notation for a month; teach me the rest on the road. The letter says the chair can only be answered by the holder, fine — but nothing in those as-builts says the holder must be alone in the antechamber, and per Rhee's own instruments I am the one mind on this continent that runs coherent next to you at depth. I'm not your stake, colleague. I'm your second. The abort line, held from inside the room.

'Two: Day One. The letter says the mooring can play the original record, and that we should watch — both of us. I've thought about it for five days, whether I can stand in that day and watch myself die and watch One make the first purchase.' The flat voice, and under it, bedrock. 'I can. I have to. Because forty-seven men watched it alone, or refused to watch it, and every one of them came out of that room still inside his own grief, and grief decided. I'll be standing next to you as the one fact none of them ever had in the room: her. Alive, informed, at full voltage, and not grateful. The engine's whole objective, standing there, telling the room what the objective actually wants.

'Three.' She picks the shirt-button queen back up, and turns it in her fingers, and looks at you across the last camp of the reckoning, and her voice does the thing it did at the beacon, cracked open on the first word and rebuilt by the fourth: 'Whatever you choose in that chair — and I heard the letter; it's yours to choose in the room; I won't decide it in advance and neither will you — you will choose it out loud, in front of me. Not for me. In front of me. That's the amendment, and it's not negotiable, and it's not the treaty talking.' A pause, one breath long, eleven years wide. 'It's me. Deal?'

choices.text: 'Deal.' And the word costs nothing, and you know exactly what it costs, and you shake on it over the board like the professionals you both pretend to be.

end_ch13.text:
The last week of the road east runs on the new footing, and the new footing holds.

Evenings, you teach her the interface grammar, and she takes it the way she takes everything — faster than you, angrier than you, annotating your annotation dialect with corrections you adopt by the third night. The state-lattice notation she learns in two sittings; the reconciliation grammar in four; and the queer beautiful convention at the heart of it all — a mind expressed as differences against a reference — she stops over, the first time she truly parses it, for a long minute. 'It's chess notation for people,' she says finally. 'Positions as divergence from a known game.' And then, quietly, working it the way she works everything, to the floor: 'And the reference game is you. Every mind in the reconciled world, written as its distance from one average man from the chess league.' She looks at you across the lamp, and does not say the next thing, and writes it instead in her own notebook, in the minute hand, on a page she does not show you — and per the treaty of witnessed hours you log its existence, 21:14, one page, unshown, and let it be: the household holds copies of everything except each other.

The country changes back around you day by day: moor to farmland, farmland to the quilted approaches, and then, from the last ridge west of the city, the whole board at once — the mismatched districts running their wrong days, the Glasshouse's iron lung glinting its lost April, the transit towers over Verstand Street, and away east, low and true and patient inside its doubled fence, the yard. The Anchorage. The still point, holding its one right day around the room where forty-seven of you lost.

B stands at the ridge line a long time, nose into the eastern wind, tail neither high nor low — his reading-the-whole-paper stance — and then he looks back at the two of you, once, as if confirming the roster.

'Inventory,' Osy says quietly, beside you, eyes on the yard. 'One wardkey. One standard, issued. One second, trained. One letter that ends where the chart does. One dog.' Her hand finds B's head without her noticing, ratifying, in the layer where the load-bearing things live. 'And one choice, colleague, waiting in a concrete room at the bottom of the world, that forty-seven versions of you carried in there already made.' She turns, and the last light off the broken city catches her face, and the cold is there and the bedrock is there and underneath them both, for the second time in two weeks, the thing you were never issued a name for. 'You'll make it in the room. Out loud. In front of me. Come on — the endgame doesn't set its own board.'
